original_abstract: Electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-derived intermediates offers a sustainable path toward propylene oxide production, avoiding the hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents typical of traditional methods. We report an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtO$_x$/C) that achieves a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% for propylene epoxidation at 50 milliamperes per square centimeter under ambient conditions. By integrating platinum into the palladium oxide crystal lattice, we stabilized high-valence platinum species, which significantly enhanced catalytic activity and selectivity. Kinetic analysis indicates that the reaction follows a mechanism of electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo (OO*) intermediates. This discovery provides a versatile strategy for the selective transfer of oxygen atoms from water without the need for chemical mediators, opening new possibilities for various sustainable oxygenation reactions.
humanized_abstract: The production of propylene oxide (PO) via the electrolysis of propylene using water-derived intermediates is a promising approach for sustainable production of PO. In contrast to current commercial processes that utilize chlorinated hydrocarbons or peroxides, chlorine-containing gases, and acids, the use of water-derived intermediates in electrolytic propylene oxidation appears to hold a greener prospect. Here, we demonstrate an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst, namely, PdPtO$_x$/C, which affords a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% for PO at the cathode at an applied current density of 50 mA cm$^{-2}$ at ambient conditions. The incorporation of platinum into the crystal lattice of palladium oxide helps to maintain high-valence platinum species, which are believed to be critical for their unique functionality toward improving the catalytic performance. Analysis of the reaction kinetics further confirmed that the catalytic conversion of propylene occurs via an electrophilic attack of OO* species. Overall, the versatility of this new concept allows for the selective transfer of oxygen atoms from water to substrates in the absence of any precursors or catalysts, thus paving the way for sustainable oxygenation processes.

The incorporation of platinum into the crystal lattice of palladium oxide helps to maintain high-valence platinum species, which are believed to be critical for their unique functionality toward improving the catalytic performance.
